title: Thai Family Words Don't Work Like English (Here's Why)

vocab.explanation: พี่ means older sibling — but it's not just for family. Thai people use พี่ to address anyone older than them, even strangers. At a restaurant you might call the server พี่ to be polite. There's no separate word for older brother versus older sister built into พี่ — you add ชาย for male (พี่ชาย, older brother) or สาว for female (พี่สาว, older sister) when you need to be specific.

exampleSentences.thai: พี่สาวของฉันสวยมากค่ะ
exampleSentences.translit: phîi-saao khǎawng chǎn sǔuai mâak khâ
exampleSentences.english: My older sister is very beautiful.
exampleSentences.note: พี่สาว is a compound: พี่ (older) + สาว (female). The gender marker always follows พี่.

vocab.explanation: น้อง is the exact counterpart to พี่ — it means younger sibling, or any younger person. Same deal: no gender built in. น้องชาย is younger brother, น้องสาว is younger sister. Just like พี่ works as a social address for older people, น้อง can address younger people too. The key insight: Thai draws the sibling line at older versus younger, not male versus female.

exampleSentences.thai: น้องชายของฉันชอบเล่นเกมค่ะ
exampleSentences.translit: náawng-chaai khǎawng chǎn châawp lên geem khâ
exampleSentences.english: My younger brother likes playing games.
exampleSentences.note: น้องชาย — younger, male. Exactly the same compound pattern as พี่ชาย.

vocab.explanation: ลุง means uncle — but specifically a male relative who is older than your parent. Either side of the family. So your father's older brother and your mother's older brother are both ลุง. In English we just say 'uncle' for everyone, but Thai keeps track of where they sit relative to your parent's generation. Older than your parent and male? That's ลุง.

exampleSentences.thai: ลุงของฉันเป็นคนขับรถค่ะ
exampleSentences.translit: lung khǎawng chǎn bpen khon khàp rót khâ
exampleSentences.note: A different context from the main conversation — same word ลุง, but talking about occupation rather than location.

vocab.explanation: ป้า is the female counterpart to ลุง — your aunt who is older than your parent, on either side of the family. You'll also hear ป้า used to politely address older women you don't know — at the market, in the neighbourhood, on the street. It acknowledges their life stage and shows warmth. If you're not sure what to call an older woman, ป้า is often a safe and respectful choice.

exampleSentences.thai: ป้าของฉันขายของที่ตลาดค่ะ
exampleSentences.translit: bpâa khǎawng chǎn khǎai khǎawng thîi tà-làat khâ
exampleSentences.english: My aunt sells things at the market.
exampleSentences.note: A different context — ป้า at the market. The word is the same regardless of which parent's side she belongs to.

vocab.english: uncle or aunt (younger than your parent, father's side)
vocab.explanation: อา covers your father's younger sibling — male or female. So a younger uncle and a younger aunt on your dad's side are both อา. This is where Thai family vocabulary really differs from English: Thai splits younger relatives by which parent they belong to. Father's younger sibling? อา. Mother's younger sibling? That's our next word, น้า. The older relatives — ลุง and ป้า — don't make this parent-side split.

exampleSentences.thai: อาของฉันอายุสามสิบปีค่ะ
exampleSentences.translit: aa khǎawng chǎn aa-yú sǎam-sìp bpii khâ
exampleSentences.english: My uncle or aunt on my father's side is thirty years old.
exampleSentences.note: อา can be male or female — context or a name usually clarifies. Very different from ลุง, which is always male and always older than your parent.

vocab.english: uncle or aunt (younger than your parent, mother's side)
vocab.explanation: น้า is the counterpart to อา — your mother's younger sibling, male or female. So if your mum has a younger brother, he's your น้า. If she has a younger sister, she's also your น้า. Notice the tones: náa has a rising tone — compare that with náawng (น้อง, younger sibling), also rising but with a longer vowel. They live in the same tonal neighbourhood, so watch the vowel length carefully.

exampleSentences.thai: น้าของฉันอยู่ที่กรุงเทพค่ะ
exampleSentences.translit: náa khǎawng chǎn yùu thîi grung-thêep khâ
exampleSentences.english: My aunt or uncle on my mum's side lives in Bangkok.
exampleSentences.note: Same อยู่ที่ pattern as the ลุง example — swap the family word and keep the structure.

vocab.explanation: ครอบครัว means family — your immediate or extended family as a unit. It's a compound: ครอบ means to surround or encompass, and ครัว means kitchen or household. So the Thai word for family literally comes from 'the people who surround the household'. That feels very fitting for a culture where family is at the centre of daily life. Three syllables — khrâawp-khruua — with a falling tone on the first and mid on the second.

exampleSentences.thai: ครอบครัวของฉันอยู่ที่โคราชค่ะ
exampleSentences.translit: khrâawp-khruua khǎawng chǎn yùu thîi khoo-râat khâ
exampleSentences.note: Korat — Nakhon Ratchasima — is a major city in northeastern Thailand. Thai families are spread all over the country, not just Bangkok.

vocab.explanation: ญาติ means relative or relatives — the same word covers singular and plural in Thai. You'll hear ญาติ constantly around Songkran, the Thai New Year in April, because it's the biggest family reunion holiday in Thailand. People travel from all over the country to be with their ญาติ. One pronunciation note: the ญ at the start is silent — you just begin with the yâat sound.

exampleSentences.thai: ญาติของฉันมาเยี่ยมทุกปีค่ะ
exampleSentences.translit: yâat khǎawng chǎn maa yîiam thúk bpii khâ
exampleSentences.english: My relatives come to visit every year.
exampleSentences.note: มา means to come, เยี่ยม means to visit, ทุกปี means every year. This is exactly what Songkran looks like across Thailand.

blocks.speakerNote: A = foreign friend visiting Nine, curious about Thai family terms, slightly puzzled expression. B = Nine, relaxed and warm, talking about her own family naturally. Visual cue: A wears a light-coloured casual shirt with a slightly unsure posture; B wears a darker top, hair down, natural and at ease. Voice modulation: A sounds tentative and curious; B is warm and conversational. Recording note: This block is the opening clip — record it as a continuous take that can be lifted as a 30-second short.

lines.english: Talking about family in Thai — it sounds simple, until you actually try it.
lines.speaker: narrator

lines.thai: ในครอบครัวเธอมีใครบ้าง?
lines.translit: nai khrâawp-khruua thooe mii khrai bâang
lines.english: Who's in your family?
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: มีพี่สองคนกับน้องสาวหนึ่งคนค่ะ
lines.translit: mii phîi sǎawng khon gàp náawng-saao nùeng khon khâ
lines.english: I have two older siblings and one younger sister.
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: พี่ชายหรือพี่สาว?
lines.translit: phîi-chaai rǔue phîi-saao
lines.english: Older brother or older sister?
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: พี่ชายหนึ่งคน พี่สาวหนึ่งคนค่ะ
lines.translit: phîi-chaai nùeng khon phîi-saao nùeng khon khâ
lines.english: One older brother, one older sister.
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: แล้วลุงป้าล่ะ? มีไหม?
lines.translit: láaeo lung bpâa lâ mii mǎi
lines.english: What about uncles and aunts? Do you have any?
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: มีเยอะมากเลยค่ะ ครอบครัวใหญ่มาก
lines.translit: mii yóe mâak looei khâ khrâawp-khruua yài mâak
lines.english: So many! Very big family.
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: โห... ญาติเยอะขนาดนั้นเลยเหรอ?
lines.translit: ooh yâat yóe khà-nàat nán looei rǒoe
lines.english: Wow... that many relatives?
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: ปกติครอบครัวไทยใหญ่แบบนี้แหละค่ะ
lines.translit: bpòk-kà-tì khrâawp-khruua thai yài bàaep níi làe khâ
lines.english: That's just normal — Thai families are this big.
lines.speaker: B

blocks.speakerNote:
Three alternative cultural-context openers for Nine to choose from during recording:

1. [DEFAULT — linguistic puzzle / one-rule unlock, used in main lines]: In English you have two words for siblings — brother and sister. In Thai you also have two words, but they split by age, not gender. That one difference changes everything about how Thai family conversations work.

2. [Direct English-to-Thai contrast]: The English word 'uncle' covers four completely different people — your mum's older brother, your mum's younger brother, your dad's older brother, your dad's younger brother. In Thai, each of those has a different word. And once you understand the logic, it all clicks immediately.

3. [Counterintuitive observation]: Most learners expect Thai to have fewer family words than English, or the same words just in a different order. The surprise is that Thai has more — and the split isn't formal versus informal, it's older versus younger. That relative-age rule runs through the entire vocabulary system.

lines.english: That conversation just now — she was talking about her siblings and her extended family. By the end of this video, you'll be able to follow every single word of that.

Here's the thing about Thai family words that nobody tells you upfront. In English you have two words for siblings — brother and sister. In Thai you also have two words. But they split by age, not by gender. พี่ is any older sibling. น้อง is any younger sibling. That one rule unlocks half this vocabulary.

And it goes even further. For aunts and uncles, Thai has four words where English only has two — because Thai also tracks whether that relative is older or younger than your parent. Once you see the pattern, everything else falls into place.
By the end of this video, you'll be able to talk about your family in Thai, use the right word for each relative, and understand why Thai family words work the way they do — all in Thai.

Today I'm going to teach you eight words for talking about family. I'll go through each one, explain what it means and how to use it, then you'll hear them all in a real conversation, and finally we'll practise together.
Let's start with the vocabulary. I'm going to go through each word one by one. For each one, I'll say the Thai, explain what it means, and show you how to use it in a sentence. Ready? Let's go.

lines.translit: phîi

lines.english: Our first word is พี่ — phîi. This means older sibling. But here's the counter-intuitive part: it doesn't tell you whether it's a brother or a sister. พี่ just means the older one. If you need to specify, you add ชาย for male — พี่ชาย, older brother — or สาว for female — พี่สาว, older sister. And there's a bonus: Thai people use พี่ to address anyone older than them, even strangers. At a restaurant, calling the server พี่ is warm and polite — they'll appreciate it.

lines.thai: พี่สาวของฉันสวยมากค่ะ
lines.translit: phîi-saao khǎawng chǎn sǔuai mâak khâ

lines.english: That means 'my older sister is very beautiful'. พี่สาว — older, female. The gender marker always goes right after พี่.
OK, next word — and it's the perfect pair to the first one.

lines.thai: น้อง
lines.translit: náawng

lines.english: น้อง — náawng — is younger sibling. The exact counterpart to พี่. Same pattern: no gender built in. น้องชาย is younger brother, น้องสาว is younger sister. This is the core insight I want you to take from these first two words: Thai draws the sibling line at older versus younger, not male versus female. Once that clicks, you'll never mix up พี่ and น้อง again.

lines.thai: น้องชายของฉันชอบเล่นเกมค่ะ
lines.translit: náawng-chaai khǎawng chǎn châawp lên geem khâ

lines.english: That means 'my younger brother likes playing games'. น้องชาย — younger, male. Exactly the same compound pattern as พี่ชาย.
Now we're moving to uncles and aunts — and this is where the Thai system gets really interesting.

lines.thai: ลุง
lines.translit: lung

lines.english: ลุง — lung — is uncle, but specifically a male relative who is older than your parent. Either side of the family. So your father's older brother and your mother's older brother are both ลุง. In English we say 'uncle' for everyone. Thai keeps track of where they sit in the generational tree. Mid tone, no mark — lung. It's one of the easier ones to remember.

lines.thai: ลุงของฉันเป็นคนขับรถค่ะ
lines.translit: lung khǎawng chǎn bpen khon khàp rót khâ

lines.english: My uncle is a driver. ของฉัน means 'my' and comes after the noun. เป็น means 'is'. คนขับรถ means driver — literally 'person who drives vehicles'.
And the female version of ลุง is...

lines.thai: ป้า
lines.translit: bpâa

lines.english: ป้า — bpâa — is aunt, specifically a female relative who is older than your parent, either side of the family. You'll also hear ป้า used to politely address older women you don't know — at the market, in the neighbourhood. It's a warm, respectful term that acknowledges someone's life stage. If you're not sure what to call an older woman in Thailand, ป้า is usually a safe and appreciated choice.

lines.thai: ป้าของฉันขายของที่ตลาดค่ะ
lines.translit: bpâa khǎawng chǎn khǎai khǎawng thîi tà-làat khâ

lines.english: My aunt sells things at the market. ขายของ is 'to sell things'. ที่ตลาด is 'at the market' — ที่ is a location marker we'll see a lot today.
OK, now the younger side. These next two are the ones that really show the Thai system at work.

lines.thai: อา
lines.translit: aa

lines.english: อา — aa — covers your father's younger sibling, male or female. Younger uncle or younger aunt on your dad's side: both are อา. This is the first of two words for younger relatives — and the split is by parent's side. Father's side is อา, mother's side is น้า. You only need that parent-side distinction for the younger relatives. For ลุง and ป้า, it doesn't matter which parent they belong to.

lines.thai: อาของฉันอายุสามสิบปีค่ะ
lines.translit: aa khǎawng chǎn aa-yú sǎam-sìp bpii khâ

lines.english: My uncle or aunt on my father's side is thirty years old. อายุ means age, สามสิบปี is thirty years. อา can be male or female — context or a name will usually clarify.
And the mother's side equivalent is...

lines.thai: น้า
lines.translit: náa

lines.english: น้า — náa — is your mother's younger sibling, male or female. Mum's younger brother? น้า. Mum's younger sister? Also น้า. Pay attention to the tone here: náa, rising tone. Compare that with náawng — น้อง, also rising but with a longer vowel. Two words, similar tones, very different meanings — so watch that vowel length.

lines.thai: น้าของฉันอยู่ที่กรุงเทพค่ะ
lines.translit: náa khǎawng chǎn yùu thîi grung-thêep khâ

lines.english: My aunt or uncle on my mum's side lives in Bangkok. Same อยู่ที่ pattern as before — person + อยู่ที่ + place means 'person lives in place'.
Now the word that holds the whole family together.

lines.thai: ครอบครัว
lines.translit: khrâawp-khruua

lines.english: ครอบครัว — khrâawp-khruua — means family. It's a compound word: ครอบ means to surround or encompass, and ครัว means kitchen or household. So the Thai word for family literally comes from 'the people who surround the household'. That feels very right for a culture where family is central to daily life. Three syllables — take your time with it. Falling tone on the first syllable, mid on the second.

lines.thai: ครอบครัวของฉันอยู่ที่โคราชค่ะ
lines.translit: khrâawp-khruua khǎawng chǎn yùu thîi khoo-râat khâ

lines.english: My family lives in Korat — Nakhon Ratchasima, a major city in northeastern Thailand. Thai families are spread all over the country, not just Bangkok, and this is a good sentence pattern to practise.
And our last word — this one you'll hear constantly around every Thai holiday.

lines.thai: ญาติ
lines.translit: yâat

lines.english: ญาติ — yâat — means relative or relatives. In Thai the same word covers singular and plural — context tells you which. You'll hear ญาติ a lot around Songkran, Thai New Year in April. It's the biggest family reunion holiday in Thailand and people travel from all over the country to be with their ญาติ. Pronunciation note: the ญ at the start is silent — you just start with yâat.

lines.thai: ญาติของฉันมาเยี่ยมทุกปีค่ะ
lines.translit: yâat khǎawng chǎn maa yîiam thúk bpii khâ

lines.english: My relatives come to visit every year. มา means to come, เยี่ยม means to visit, ทุกปี means every year. This is exactly what Songkran looks like across Thailand.

OK, so now you know all eight words. What I'm going to do is put them together into a short conversation — imagine you're getting to know a Thai friend and she's telling you about her family. I'm going to speak at natural speed, the way a Thai person would actually talk. Don't pause the video. Just listen and see how much you can understand. Ready? Here we go.

lines.thai: พี่ชายของฉันชื่อต้นค่ะ
น้องสาวของฉันยังเรียนอยู่ค่ะ
ลุงของฉันอยู่ที่เชียงใหม่ค่ะ
ป้าของฉันทำอาหารเก่งมากค่ะ
ครอบครัวของฉันใหญ่มากค่ะ
ญาติของฉันอยู่ทั่วประเทศค่ะ

lines.english: How was that? Could you catch some of those phrases? Don't worry if you didn't get everything — that's completely normal. Now let's break it down.
I'm going to go through that same conversation again, but this time sentence by sentence. I'll say the Thai, and then I'll explain exactly what each part means.

lines.thai: พี่ชายของฉันชื่อต้นค่ะ
lines.translit: phîi-chaai khǎawng chǎn chûue tôn khâ
phîi-chaai khǎawng chǎn chûue tôn khâ

lines.english: My older brother's name is Ton.

พี่ชาย — older brother. ของฉัน — of me, my — it comes right after the noun it belongs to. ชื่อ — is named. ต้น — that's his name. Notice the word order: Thai says 'older-brother of-me named Ton', not 'My older brother is named Ton'. The possessive always follows the noun in Thai.

lines.thai: น้องสาวของฉันยังเรียนอยู่ค่ะ
lines.translit: náawng-saao khǎawng chǎn yang rian yùu khâ
náawng-saao khǎawng chǎn yang rian yùu khâ

lines.english: My younger sister is still studying.

น้องสาว — younger sister. ยัง means 'still'. เรียน means to study. อยู่ here isn't 'lives' — it's acting as a continuous aspect marker, a bit like English '-ing'. So เรียนอยู่ means 'is studying' or 'still studying'. อยู่ doing this kind of double duty is very common in Thai — the same word carries different meanings depending on how it's used.

lines.thai: ลุงของฉันอยู่ที่เชียงใหม่ค่ะ
lines.translit: lung khǎawng chǎn yùu thîi chiang-mài khâ
lung khǎawng chǎn yùu thîi chiang-mài khâ

lines.english: My uncle lives in Chiang Mai.

ลุง — uncle, older than your parent. อยู่ here means 'lives' or 'is located'. ที่ signals that a place name is coming. เชียงใหม่ — Chiang Mai, in northern Thailand. อยู่ที่ together is a really useful pattern: person + อยู่ที่ + place = 'person lives in place'. You can swap in any family word and any location.

lines.thai: ป้าของฉันทำอาหารเก่งมากค่ะ
lines.translit: bpâa khǎawng chǎn tham aa-hǎan gèng mâak khâ
bpâa khǎawng chǎn tham aa-hǎan gèng mâak khâ

lines.english: My aunt cooks really well.

ป้า — aunt, older than your parent. ทำอาหาร means to cook — literally 'do food'. เก่ง means skilled or good at something. มาก means very or a lot. So เก่งมาก is 'very skilled'. Notice the order: เก่งมาก, not มากเก่ง. Intensifiers like มาก come after the word they modify in Thai — same direction as English actually.

lines.thai: ครอบครัวของฉันใหญ่มากค่ะ
lines.translit: khrâawp-khruua khǎawng chǎn yài mâak khâ
khrâawp-khruua khǎawng chǎn yài mâak khâ

lines.english: My family is very big.

ครอบครัว — family. ของฉัน — my. ใหญ่ means big or large. มาก means very. There's no verb 'is' in this sentence — in Thai, adjectives can follow a noun directly without a linking verb. ครอบครัวของฉันใหญ่ reads literally as 'my-family big', and it's perfectly natural Thai.

lines.thai: ญาติของฉันอยู่ทั่วประเทศค่ะ
lines.translit: yâat khǎawng chǎn yùu thûua bprà-thêet khâ
yâat khǎawng chǎn yùu thûua bprà-thêet khâ

lines.english: My relatives are all over the country.

ญาติ — relatives. อยู่ — are located. ทั่ว means throughout or all over. ประเทศ means country. So อยู่ทั่วประเทศ is 'scattered throughout the country'. This is a very natural thing to say in Thailand — families really are spread out, and Songkran is the holiday that brings everyone back together.

Now it's your turn to practise. I'm going to say each sentence slowly, and I want you to repeat after me. Don't be shy — say it out loud! Speaking is how you learn. I'll leave a gap after each sentence for you to repeat.

lines.thai: พี่ชายของฉันชื่อต้นค่ะ
lines.thaiSplit: พี่ชาย ของ ฉัน ชื่อ ต้น ค่ะ
lines.translit: phîi-chaai khǎawng chǎn chûue tôn khâ

lines.thai: น้องสาวของฉันยังเรียนอยู่ค่ะ
lines.thaiSplit: น้องสาว ของ ฉัน ยัง เรียน อยู่ ค่ะ
lines.translit: náawng-saao khǎawng chǎn yang rian yùu khâ

lines.thai: ลุงของฉันอยู่ที่เชียงใหม่ค่ะ
lines.thaiSplit: ลุง ของ ฉัน อยู่ ที่ เชียงใหม่ ค่ะ
lines.translit: lung khǎawng chǎn yùu thîi chiang-mài khâ

lines.thai: ป้าของฉันทำอาหารเก่งมากค่ะ
lines.thaiSplit: ป้า ของ ฉัน ทำ อาหาร เก่ง มาก ค่ะ
lines.translit: bpâa khǎawng chǎn tham aa-hǎan gèng mâak khâ

lines.thai: ครอบครัวของฉันใหญ่มากค่ะ
lines.thaiSplit: ครอบครัว ของ ฉัน ใหญ่ มาก ค่ะ
lines.translit: khrâawp-khruua khǎawng chǎn yài mâak khâ

lines.thai: ญาติของฉันอยู่ทั่วประเทศค่ะ
lines.thaiSplit: ญาติ ของ ฉัน อยู่ ทั่ว ประเทศ ค่ะ
lines.translit: yâat khǎawng chǎn yùu thûua bprà-thêet khâ

lines.english: Great job! If that felt hard, that's totally fine — rewind and do it again. Repetition is how you get better.
OK, quiz time! I'm going to give you a situation in English, and you need to say the Thai phrase. I'll give you a few seconds to think, and then I'll give you the answer. Ready?

blocks.speakerNote: Look to camera. Ask the question, then wait 3 seconds in silence.

lines.english: You want to say 'My older brother lives in Chiang Mai.' How do you say that in Thai?
Try saying it now...

lines.thai: พี่ชายของฉันอยู่ที่เชียงใหม่ค่ะ
lines.translit: phîi-chaai khǎawng chǎn yùu thîi chiang-mài khâ

lines.english: That's right — พี่ชายของฉันอยู่ที่เชียงใหม่ means 'My older brother lives in Chiang Mai.' พี่ชาย for older brother, อยู่ที่ for lives in, then the place.

blocks.speakerNote: Look to camera. Ask the question, then wait 3 seconds in silence.

lines.english: How would you say 'My family is very big' in Thai?
Try saying it now...

lines.thai: ครอบครัวของฉันใหญ่มากค่ะ
lines.translit: khrâawp-khruua khǎawng chǎn yài mâak khâ

lines.english: That's right — ครอบครัวของฉันใหญ่มาก means 'My family is very big.' ครอบครัว for family, ใหญ่มาก for very big — adjective first, then มาก to intensify.

blocks.speakerNote: Look to camera. Ask the question, then wait 3 seconds in silence.

lines.english: Your mum's younger sister cooks really well. What do you call her in Thai, and how would you say she cooks well?
Try saying it now...

lines.thai: น้าของฉันทำอาหารเก่งมากค่ะ
lines.translit: náa khǎawng chǎn tham aa-hǎan gèng mâak khâ

lines.english: That's right — น้าของฉันทำอาหารเก่งมาก means 'My aunt on mum's side cooks really well.' The key word is น้า — mother's younger sibling, not ป้า, because she's younger than your mum.

blocks.speakerNote: Look to camera. Ask the question, then wait 3 seconds in silence.

lines.english: How do you say 'My relatives are all over the country' in Thai?
Try saying it now...

lines.thai: ญาติของฉันอยู่ทั่วประเทศค่ะ
lines.translit: yâat khǎawng chǎn yùu thûua bprà-thêet khâ

lines.english: That's right — ญาติของฉันอยู่ทั่วประเทศ means 'My relatives are all over the country.' ญาติ for relatives, อยู่ทั่วประเทศ for all over the country.
Before we finish, let's quickly go over everything you learned today.
Let's do a quick recap of everything you learned today.

The big takeaway: Thai family words encode age and generation, not just gender. พี่ and น้อง split by older versus younger. ลุง and ป้า are for relatives older than your parent. อา and น้า are for younger relatives — father's side and mother's side. Once you see the system, it all makes sense. Thanks for watching! If this helped you, give it a thumbs up and subscribe so you don't miss the next one.

blocks.speakerNote: Pre-Rev-6 — may need retro-fit when template updates.

lines.english: In the next episode, we're going to talk about daily activities — the things you do every day in Thai. Here's a little preview:

lines.thai: ทำอะไรทุกวัน?
lines.translit: tham a-rai thúk wan
lines.english: What do you do every day?
